data.title: Laughing Water Capital Q1 2026 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners,

During the first quarter of 2026 a Class A investment in Laughing Water Capital returned approximately -4.5% net of all fees and expenses. i During the quarter, the SP500TR and R2000 returned -4.3% and 0.9% respectively. As always, please check your individual statements for the most accurate measure of your individual return, which may vary based on fund, class, and timing. As a reminder, our portfolio is completely different than the indexes, so there is no reason for us to think that our performance will be similar to the indexes over shorter periods of time. However, over longer periods of time I expect we will be rewarded by taking the road less travelled. On this front, during the quarter Laughing Water Capital passed the ten year mark since inception. Over the first ten years of Laughing Water's existence, returns were approximately 410%, or 17.7% per year, versus 339% or 15.9% annualized for the SP500TR and 189.2% or 11.2% annualized for our most relevant benchmark, the R2000. ii

Despite this past success, the future is of course what matters. Of particular note, at present our portfolio is more concentrated than it has been historically, meaning the short-term fluctuations are likely to be more extreme. The reason for this increased concentration is increased confidence in the near and intermediate term prospects of several of our holdings. Each of these holdings trades day to day based on the vagaries of the market and the quantitative read of their financial statements. However, I believe each of them has value far in excess of what a cursory review would suggest, and I believe this value will be revealed shortly through a series of idiosyncratic events that should take place regardless of what else is happening in the world. I believe that bearing the immediate term volatility will be worth it for the intermediate term reward.

I would also note that this past quarter has been eventful for the composition of our portfolio as several longer held positions have been reduced or exited, while several new positions have taken their place. Some part of these changes are tied to negative developments in legacy positions, but the over-riding influence has simply been finding better opportunities. In this case "better" means stronger balance sheets, better competitive positions, more predictable event paths, less reliance on macro or industry forces, and at times cheaper valuations. I believe the next ten years for Laughing Water Capital can be as successful as the last ten years, and for this reason almost the entirety of my and my family's wealth remains invested right alongside yours. Our interests are aligned.

Where Are We Now?

“We didn’t start the fire - It was always burning since the world’s been turning”

Events of the last three months including advancements in artificial intelligence, U.S. interventions in Venezuela and Iran, and resultant rising oil prices would fit perfectly as a verse in Billy Joel’s classic song that recounts the major events of much of the second half of the 20 th century. I think it is important however to remember that the point of the song is that crazy things have always been happening, and the world just keeps on spinning. For us, this means that we should mostly ignore the global-macro-political noise, and instead stay focused on the prospects of our individual companies.

That being said, volatility creates opportunities, and there have been several changes to our portfolio in recent months. Most notably, over the last few years much of our portfolio has been in names where the thesis was often multi-year in nature. Further, value was not always apparent without significant diligence, and longer timelines meant that ultimate value was subject to increased event path risk.

In contrast, the latest additions to our portfolio are characterized by shorter timelines, value that is underpinned by rock solid balance sheets and near term cash flows, and limited event path risk. I will share more thoughts on this evolution in future communications, but in many ways this is simply me leaning into what has worked better for us in the past, and leaning away from the type of investments that have powered much of the markets returns in recent years.

New Positions

Undisclosed – We have a new investment in our Top 5 that will remain undisclosed for now as I plan to increase the position size. In brief, this investment is in a “good co / bad co” situation, where one company operates two segments, one of which is very attractive, and one of which is less attractive. The good co has a dominant competitive position and generates a lot of cash. The combined company has an unlevered balance sheet and trades at ~10x my estimate of normalized FCF. Recent changes to the board and management, as well as the arrival of an activist, suggest that the good co will be allowed to shine in the not too distant future. Please stay tuned.

Stride Inc. (LRN) -Stride entered our portfolio as a mid-sized position. The company is the largest operator of virtual schools for grades K-12 in the U.S., serving more than 240,000 students across 30 states. Virtual schools are enjoying secular tail winds as advances in technology (Zoom, Google Meet etc.) and changes to the way online education is perceived following the Covid experience have led to what I believe is a sustainable increase in demand.

The company had been growing at mid to low teens percent for several years and traded at ~14x EBIT when they guided to 10-15% growth in enrollment for fiscal '26. However, prior to the 2025-2026 school year the company attempted to upgrade the software that governs their enrollment process as well as their learning management software. The implementation did not go smoothly, and in late October of 2025 management announced they would be cutting guidance. In brief, the new software was disjointed to the point that parents could not successfully navigate the enrollment process, and if successful, students faced problems with logging in and finding the right classes and curriculum. This cost the company upwards of 10,000 enrollments and led to a more than 50% decline in share price, where we began to buy stock. In response to these events, management initiated a $500M buyback and moved quickly to fix the software problems.

Given that commentary from both Stride and a key competitor indicates that demand for virtual schools remains strong, this investment logically comes down to one issue; either they can fix their software, or they cannot. At present, the company is valued below 7.5x EBIT, which seems to imply either 1) that the company will not be able to fix their software or 2) that the market does not have the patience to wait for the company to fix their software. I am of the view that given enough time, the problems will be fixed, and in fact early indications from both the company and various open market sources are that the company is well on its way down this path. As the fixes take hold, I expect shares to re-rate closer to where they traded prior to the sell off.

It is also important to note that unlike many for profit education companies that have left a sour taste in Wall Street's mouth in the past, Stride is NOT reliant on students taking on loans that they cannot afford in order to attend school. Rather, Stride enters into long term contracts with school districts who fund the students' education.

Theravance Biopharma, Inc. (TBPH) -In American football, if the defense goes offsides but the play still starts, the offense has a tremendous advantage. If they execute a successful play, the play stands. If they are unsuccessful, they are not penalized for the attempt due to the offsides call. The offense is thus incentivized to go for the end zone.

Theravance is a special situation that fits this framework. A longer write up can be found at the end of this letter, but in brief, following a failed Phase III FDA trial the company is a pile of cash and similar attached to a royalty stream that should generate ~$65M per year for the Company, as well as $2.6B in tax assets. The company has announced that they have accelerated the work of a strategic review committee, essentially saying that the royalty stream is for sale. It is hard to know exactly what a buyer will pay, but the downside is well protected by cash and near cash, and anything from $17-$25 is well within reason. Under certain scenarios, a buyer may be willing to pay a few dollars more. We bought our shares below $14, and in my view, the downside protection and range of upside outcomes is such that we should go for the end zone in terms of position sizing. Theravance is thus a top 5 position for us. I expect this situation to resolve itself within the next 0-6 months.

Also of note:

Nextnav Inc (NN) – I have written about Nextnav, our wireless spectrum / 5G terrestrial backup to GPS investment, several times in the past. This investment remains an outsized position, and it remains subject to wild swings every month around the timing of the release of each month's Federal Communications Commission (FCC) agenda. The swings can be frustrating, but in my view, the investment has been largely de-risked as the FCC submitted a draft Notice of Proposed Rule Making (NPRM) to the White House Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (OIRA).

In other words, it seems that the FCC is now on our side.

However, the proposal still needs to pass OIRA review, which would entail checking with other agencies that could be affected by the proposal. In this case, that would primarily be the Department of Transportation (DOT) and the Department of Defense (DOD). From my perspective, it seems unlikely that FCC Chair Brendan Carr would submit a proposal to OIRA without first running it by DOT and DOD. Further, the DOT and FCC both fall under oversight from the Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee, which is chaired by Senator Ted Cruz. Cruz has been a vocal advocate for terrestrial GPS in the past, which seems to indicate that the DOT will be on board.

The DOD's spectrum usage is overseen by the National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA), The NTIA is part of the Department of Commerce under Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick. Lutnick in the past has said that the government has too much spectrum, and it should be freed up for better use cases. Additionally, a backup to GPS is indeed something that the DOD wants and needs.

Lastly, recall that it was Trump who started the whole push for a backup to GPS/PNT with an executive order at the end of his first term, so it seems unlikely that an arm of the executive branch would now torpedo Nextnav's proposal.

The timing here has admittedly stretched a few months longer than I hoped for, but I think we are almost there, and I expect to see an NPRM shortly. Following an NPRM there would be a comment period that could stretch to 60 days from the time the NPRM is posted in the Federal Registrar. At this point much of what could be said has already been said during the Notice of Inquiry phase of this preceding, so I am hopeful comments will move quickly. From there I would expect a Report and Order which will allow Nextnav to use their spectrum for 5G communications and a terrestrial backup to GPS.

To be clear, we still do not know what will be in an NPRM, and it is possible that Nextnav will not get everything they have asked for, which would of course weigh on the value of the spectrum. However, the entire point of this exercise is to incentivize the mobile network operators (VZ, T, TMUS) or other interested parties to roll out a terrestrial backup to GPS nationwide. In order to properly motivate them, the FCC must ensure that the spectrum in question is attractively configured. If the spectrum is attractively configured, it should be quite valuable.

“How valuable” is an open question, but recent transactions suggest something north of $50 (vs. month end price of ~$15) is well within range. It is also worth noting that there is a history of spectrum pricing exceeding the top end of expectations during competitive bid processes. Recent news that Amazon (AMZN) is interested in terrestrial spectrum through Globalstar (GSAT) and that SpaceX is looking to acquire more spectrum can only be viewed as positive. Afterall, all that is needed to push value higher in an auction process of a scarce asset is two interested buyers. The arrival of two likely bidders with very deep pockets could push the price significantly higher.

Liquidia Corp (LQDA) -Liquidia is our maker of a drug called YUTREPIA that combats Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) and Pulmonary Hypertension Interstitial Lung Disease (PH-ILD). The sales launch of this drug continues to exceed all expectations, and the stock has appreciated nicely. However, I believe shares would be much higher if not for an on-going patent battle with United Therapeutics (UTHR). Legal arguments in the last round concluded last summer, but the judge has not yet ruled. My work around the opinions of legal experts and past rulings in similar cases by this judge suggest a favorable outcome is on the way. The delay is of course frustrating, but I think the likely explanation is that the judge is taking the time to write an opinion that would be bullet proof on appeal. Logically, if he were going to rule against Liquidia he would do so quickly, because every day that he has not ruled is another day that more patients are put on YUTREPIA, and historically judges have not wanted to pull medication from people whose lives are improved by it.

Importantly, this trial is only tied to the use of YUTREPIA to treat PH-ILD. In other words, even if the ruling is unfavorable Liquidia should be able to continue to take share in the PAH market. This should provide downside protection for the stock. If the ruling is favorable and the launch of YUTREPIA continues unimpeded, even a material slow down in the rate of adoption since inception could lead to a revenue run rate north of $1.2B by the end of the year. If Liquidia were to trade at the same multiple of sales as United Therapeutics, shares would be worth more than double where they are today. Additionally, the major pharmaceutical companies are facing an estimated $300B in patent cliffs over the next few years. These same companies have an estimated $1T in dry powder to make acquisitions in order to fill in the looming patent hole. Liquidia appears to be a prime candidate for a takeout by large pharma.

SECURE Waste Infrastructure Corp (SES-TO) – SECURE was introduced in our 1H'25 letter as a business that had transitioned from oil services to waste services tied to the Canadian oil industry. This transition, combined with wonky accounting, explained why shares were cheap, and we purchased our shares just after an undersubscribed tender offer was completed for around C$14.50. As I was about to hit “send” on this letter, news surfaced that GFL Environmental Inc. (GFL), a Canadian-American waste player that is ~6x larger than SECURE, would be purchasing SECURE for C$24.75 per share in a stock and cash deal. My initial reaction is to not be thrilled with this price.

Lifecore Biomedical Inc. (LFCR) -Lifecore, our fill finish CDMO that puts drugs into syringes and vials, continues to win new business at an impressive rate. However, the time from winning new business to generating meaningful cash flows from that business is measured in years in the CDMO world. Additionally, Lifecore recently announced that much of their pipeline is moving to the right as customer timing has shifted. As a result, the stock has been severely punished.

The shifting timeline is of course frustrating, but I think the market is over-reacting to fears of a potential capital raise as the company's series A convertible preferred shares mature this summer. However, these shares are designed to PIK at maturity if the company is unable to redeem them, so no immediate liquidity needs are on the horizon. As the business the company has already won comes in the door, the company will be well positioned to deal with this and other capital structure needs.

In my view, the gap between the public market value of LFCR stock and the private market value of Lifecore's assets has never been wider. Management deserves full credit for executing at a very high level, but in my view it is incumbent upon the board to close this gap through a sale of the company. I believe (but am not certain) that private buyers were willing to pay approximately $8 per share for Lifecore three years ago, and since that time management has taken out a lot of cost and won a lot of new business.

Snatching Defeat from the Jaws of Victory

During the quarter I exited two long held positions, both of which were large winners at one point, and both of which left the portfolio under less auspicious circumstances. While I did sell some stock along the way, in both cases it seems as if I was too slow to react to changing circumstances and I allowed good investments to turn into mediocre investments. Part of the slow reaction time is explained by my belief that both of these businesses are longer term winners. However, business execution is far from the sole contributor to stock market performance, and as it became clear that the world around these investments was changing or had changed, exiting became the best path forward. I would not be surprised if we owned these businesses again in the future.

PAR Technology Corp (PAR) – PAR was our restaurant software business. I continue to believe that PAR will win their category, but I was never completely comfortable with valuation, which for PAR and other software companies has been measured as a multiple of annual recurring revenue, or "ARR" over the last several years. This concept has gone out of favor due to the rise of AI, and it is not clear to me when it will come back in favor. Consider that during the last major software disruption in the early 2000s companies like Microsoft traded at 12x earnings. As I said, I believe that PAR will win their category, but it will be some time before customer wins translate into real earnings, and with ARR out of favor the downside protection to me in this investment was not clear. Further, there has always been an element of reflexivity tied to PAR's capital structure due to convertible debt. A high stock price allows you to refinance converts with minimal pain, while a low share price brings significant dilution. I exited our position prior to PAR raising a new convert at significantly lower conversion prices than the previous instrument.

Vistry Group PLC (VTY-L) – Vistry was our UK homebuilder that was transitioning away from traditional home building and toward an asset light “partnerships” model focused on low-income housing. As part of this transition, a year ago Vistry announced they had found accounting missteps tied to their legacy business, which led to a selloff in the shares. Nevertheless, I did not sell at that time because the key elements of the thesis were that Vistry had best in class management and would be returning a lot of capital to shareholders via share repurchases. A legacy accounting problem did not weaken the thesis.

However, on their most recent earnings call Vistry announced that CEO Greg Fitzgerald would be retiring, and that they will “continue to consider” future buybacks. Clearly, these developments impaired the thesis. That being said, the structural deficit in affordable housing in the U.K. is still a problem, and massive government funding should still be flowing through the system in the years to come. As capital allocation plans and management succession plans become more clear, Vistry will likely be worth re-visiting.

Looking Forward

As always, I have no idea what to expect in the near term for the stock market, and neither does anyone else. What I remain convinced of however is that our businesses are building value, and as long as they continue to execute, with time we will be rewarded. This is true regardless of what happens with global politics, domestic politics, oil prices, or anything else.

It may also be worth noting that during the first ten years of LWC’s existence, large cap stocks have ruled the day, while small cap stocks – where we play – have been out of favor. Historically this relationship has flip-flopped every 10 or 12 years. Recent market performance seems to suggest that a reversal may be upon us. I am fully aware of the arguments against small cap stocks, including private equity buyers picking over smaller companies and private companies delaying their IPOs. Nevertheless, the gap between large cap and small cap valuations remains near record spreads at a time when small cap earnings are expected to outpace large cap earnings going forward. iii

Of course, nothing is ever guaranteed, and ultimately our performance will be determined by my ability to pick the right investments for our partnerships. But if we were able to outperform large caps during a 10 year stretch when large caps had every advantage over our strategy, I am hopeful that a return to small cap prominence will provide us a significant tailwind over the next 10 years.

Matthew Sweeney

Managing Partner

Appendix: Theravance Biopharma Inc

Theravance Biopharma Inc (TBPH) is a special situation where the downside is well protected by cash on the balance sheet, near term milestone payments, and near-term cash flow, and the upside is likely 50-100% over the next 0-6 months.

Following the failure of a Phase 3 study and a ~30% decline in share price, the company is now ~$400M in cash, $100M in 2026 milestones tied to a sales goal that was easily exceeded in 2025, ~$2.6B in Irish tax assets, and ~$60 -$70M per year in FCF tied to a 35% profit share from sales of YUPELRI, a growing pulmonary drug that treats COPD with patent protection until 2039. YUPELRI is majority owned and commercialized by Viatris (VTRS). The $65M in FCF is pro forma for Theravance following through on already announced plans to reduce headcount and otherwise cut expenses by 60%, which is expected to be complete by Q3'26. Further, the company has announced that the work of their Strategic Review Committee is being “accelerated”. The preceding information can all be found in the 8K that the company released on 3/3/26 following the Phase 3 failure, but to sum it up, the $65M cash flow stream is for sale.

To cut to the chase, valuation can be backed into 2 ways. The first is a simple DCF which is of course sensitive to inputs.

I ignore the balance of 2026 and start in 2027 with $65,000M in FCF (as guided by the company at the mid-point), add back ~$4.5M in corporate expense (which still leaves $39M in run rate SG&A, much of which could be cut by the right buyer), and then assume growth of 5% for the next 5 years before flatlining until patent expiration and getting a 2x terminal value. I then discount back by 10%, add expected Q1'26 cash (guided by the company & no debt) of $400M, add $100M in milestone payments due Q1'27, and divide by diluted shares to get around $20 in value. This assumes zero value for the $2.6B in Irish tax assets.

A Brief Word on YUPELRI and COPD

Before defending these inputs, first a bit about YUPELRI. YUPELRI is a Long-acting Muscarinic Antagonist (LAMA). LAMAs are used to treat Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), and act as bronchodilators, meaning they help keep the airways open for an extended period (about 24 hours). YUPELRI is the only once-daily, nebulized LAMA maintenance medicine for COPD. Theravance discovered and developed revefenacin, which is the API in YUPELRI, and partnered with Mylan (which became Viatris after merging with Pfizer’s generics unit in 2020) in 2015 to develop and commercialize the drug. Viatris now leads commercialization and marketing, while Theravance co-promotes, focusing on the hospital/institutional channel in the U.S. 65% of domestic net sales go to Viatris, and 35% go to Theravance. Viatris also handles commercialization and marketing in the rest of the world, while Theravance can receive tiered royalties on sales in ROW.

LAMAs work by blocking muscarinic receptors in the smooth muscle of the airways, and are considered first-line maintenance therapy for moderate to severe COPD. LAMAs are typically just part of a treatment plan, with many patients being prescribed both a LAMA and a Long-Acting Beta Agonist (LABA) which work through a different receptor pathway than LAMAs. Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS) are also commonly used as well, and LAMA + LABA + ICS is known as triple therapy.

There are of course new potential treatment options being developed for COPD, but bronchodilators like LAMAs are expected to remain as the backbone of any treatment plan, and are unlikely to be disrupted any time in the foreseeable future.

Supporting Assumptions

Growth: YUPELRI sales grew 12% in 2025, the drug has about 5% share in a domestic market that is expected to grow 4-5% per year and was recently approved in China. Theravance will receive 14% of China sales under $75M, 17% of sales between $75M and $150M, and 20% of sales above $150M, as well as certain milestones along the way. Domestically, the company investor presentation lays out their strategy for growth (which appears to be working), but in brief according to Theravance many COPD patients are inappropriately using short-acting nebulizers 4-6x per day rather than a LAMA once a day, and many patients are relying on handheld inhalers throughout the day rather than the once daily LAMA. To be fair, this is a competitive space, which is why I am not more aggressive with growth assumptions, but it seems reasonable to think that the only once a day treatment will at least grow in line with the market -if not take share as it has been recently -over the next few years.

Discount Rate: This is all obviously finger in the air, but I start with 10% based on general commentary from industry participants, as well as this document titled “ Pharmaceutical Royalty Structures: A Global Technical Analysis. ” If you take the discount to 12%, value drops to around $18.50. At 15%, value drops to a bit below $17. The reality is that you can make a DCF say whatever you want. The point here though is that even if you use 15%, the outcome from an investment in equity at today’s prices is pretty good, especially given the likely short timeline. At 10%, the outcome is great, and it is not crazy to think that a majority partner would pay a 10% rate to lock up the asset (more on this below). It is also not crazy to think they’d discount at 8% given their familiarity with the drug and its prospects, and that gets you north of $21.

Patent Protection: There is a Composition of Matter patent that expires in 2028, a Polymorph Patent that expires in 2031, and a Method of Use patent that expires in 2039. In brief, this means that after 2028 competitors could sell generics for other uses but cannot promote it for the same use case as YUPELRI, which is maintenance treatment of COPD.

Of note, in 2022 multiple generic makers filed ANDAs, and the company has settled with all 8 of them, ensuring they will not be entering the market until 2039.

Expected milestones: Theravance previously sold their TRELEGY royalty to Royalty Pharma in 2022 while retaining the rights to outer year royalties. The outer year rights were sold to GSK (GSK) in 2025. The sales to Royalty Pharma included milestone payments which Theravance has been collecting. The most recent payment was $50M in February 2026 for exceeding 2025 sales goals with net sales of $3.91B. The FY2026 net sales goal is $3.51B, which will trigger an additional $100M royalty payment. As the 2026 goal was easily exceeded in 2025, the company considers collection of this $100M highly likely.

Additional milestone payments of up to $45M are possible depending on YUPELRI sales in China, which is the world’s largest COPD market. I give zero credit for these potential milestones.

A Second Approach to Valuation

The other way that I think about valuation is through the eyes of Viatris. Again, Viatris is the majority partner here, keeping 65% of net sales. This is a $16.5B market cap company with 1,400 different molecules across 7,500 products for sale, and a $1B buyback in place. Viatris is guiding to $2.15B in 2026 FCF. In other words, Viatris seems willing to pay at least 7.7x FCF for their own stock. As YUPELRI is growing faster than the system average and has patent protection length on par with the system average, it seems likely that Viatris would be better served by paying 7.7x for Theravance’s cut of the YUPELRI sales than they would by buying their own stock at 7.7x. If they were to pay 7.7x, Theravance would be worth approximately $19 per share.

Viatris Multiple 7.7x
FCF to Viatris $ 70,000
Value $539,000
+ cash 400,000
+ Trelogy milestone 100,000
+ options cash 29,683
+ tax assets 0
Value $1,068,683
Diluted Shares 56,393
Value / Share $ 18.95

Again I give zero credit for the Irish tax assets, and do not boost the FCF to reflect synergies that Viatris could surely recognize by eliminating the Theravance sales force and just putting YUPELRI in their existing hospital reps’ bags or having their YUPELRI field reps cover hospitals as well. Even if Viatris determined they needed to keep the reps, they could surely take cost out on the administrative side of the sales process.

How Do We Know Management Will Follow Through?

As mentioned, the company 8K'd that they will be "accelerating" the work of the company's Strategic Review Committee. This committee is the result of pressure from 2 activists that have been involved for a few years now: Irenic Capital and Weiss Asset Management. Both Irenic and Weiss have board representation and have been pushing for the company to realize value rather than fund science projects. They had previous success with Theravance's sale of TRELEGY, which is the source of the $100M milestone payment that should arrive in Q1'27. It may also be worth noting that the director who is running the strategic review committee is also the former CFO of Royalty Pharma (RPRX). RPRX could also be a potential buyer here, although that may necessitate a creative solution to the hospital sales force. In any case, 2 activists that have already had success in the boardroom have been steering the company in the direction of a sale for several years now. It is extremely likely that they follow through.

Why Won't Viatris Just Low Ball? / Other Potential Buyers?

Theravance's 35% cut of YUPELRI is a bit of an odd duck, as it is NOT a pure play royalty stream. As mentioned previously, Theravance is responsible for maintaining a hospital focused sales force to peddle YUPELRI in the hospital channel. This wrinkle could understandably prevent a purely financial buyer from being interested in the cash flow stream.

It seems obvious that Viatris is the most logical buyer – they already own 65%, they know the market, and they know the drug. However, because they are such a logical buyer there is a possibility that Viatris will only come with a low-ball bid.

The recipe to prevent a low-ball bid is of course other potential buyers. So who else might be interested?

The answer is basically anyone who is also involved in the other pieces of the "triple therapy" described above. Following the planned restructuring, TBPH will still have ~$40M of SG&A costs which supports a sales force, a sales support team, a home office, a management team, etc. etc. For any company that is already calling on pulmonologists as part of the COPD triple therapy world (or for other reasons), they could likely wipe out almost all of these costs (assuming they already have salespeople focused on the hospital/institutional channel), and simply put YUPELRI into their existing pulmonary sales forces' bag.

You can capitalize or account for these savings however you want – simply adding back another $30M of annual expense to the FCF in the DCF above will get you a value north of $24 per share. But from my perspective, it is less important to try to fully value these costs and generate an upside

case than it is to simply think that Viatris should be motivated to come in with a legitimate bid rather than a low-ball bid. Surely they don't want 35% of their profits in the hands of a competitor.

About Those Irish Tax Assets....

I am not an Irish (or domestic) tax expert. What seems clear though is that in order to be able to use the tax assets, any buyer would have to continue on in the same business line that generated the tax assets. In other words, if a buyer were simply after the YUPELRI cash flows and did not also have an R&D arm, they would likely not be able to use the assets. Most important in my mind is the fact that Viatris does in fact have a significant R&D presence in Ireland across 5 sites that employ 1,600 people. It follows then that Viatris would ascribe some value to these tax assets. Again, this is a finger in the air exercise, but if you figure that these tax assets are worth 6.25% of face (half the Irish tax rate), that gets you to around $23 based on the above valuations. Or you can assume that the 10% discount rate in the DCF above is too aggressive, and assume that a 15% discount rate would be more appropriate and still come out with a combined value of somewhere around $20.

In Conclusion

We paid an average price of $13.70 per share for our investment in Theravance. This is an investment where the downside is protected by ~$10/share of cash and near-term milestone payments, and the market is putting a ~3.5x multiple on a quasi-royalty stream that is patent protected for the next 13 years. Importantly, properly incentivized activists are in the board room and pushing for the upside, which is comfortably ~50% and as much as 100% if a buyer thinks they can take all of the cost out by dropping YUPELRI in their existing sales forces' bags and realize value from the Irish tax assets. As a Strategic Review Committee has recently "accelerated" their work and the most likely buyer would have negligible diligence requirements, it seems likely that this situation will be resolved within the next 0 to 6 months.

data.title: ACM Research: Let's Go To Hong Kong
data.description:
ACM Research (ACMR) released an 8K on Friday with very promising implications for shareholders. The company’s shares trade at a dramatic discount to the shares of its Chinese-listed subsidiary (ACMS) whose shares trade on the Shanghai STAR exchange, and market participants have long discussed ways in which this discount could narrow. Our view is that a dual listing in Hong Kong would be an effective way of narrowing the discount and is the most preferable solution. Friday’s 8K represented a step in that direction, as the company announced that its Chinese subsidiary, ACM Shanghai, “issued an announcement” to the Shanghai Stock Exchange “that ACM Shanghai is proposing to issue overseas listed shares and apply for listing on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited (the “H Share Listing”).”

ACMR, the U.S.-listed ticker of ACM Research, Inc., trades at a massive discount to ACMS, its Chinese-listed subsidiary ACM Research (Shanghai), Inc. The market capitalization of ACMR is $3.6b. It owns 74% of ACMS, with the remaining 26% of ACMS owned by Chinese shareholders. ACMS is being valued at $11.1b based on the value of its Chinese shares in the Chinese stock market currently, rendering the value of ACMR’s 74% stake to be $8.1b. So the US stock market is valuing ACMR’s market valuation at $3.6b, while it owns paper worth $8.1b. This shareholding is fully transactable—in fact, on February 6, 2026, ACMR sold a token 1% of its shareholdings at ¥160 per ACMS share for proceeds of $111 million. The transaction price of ¥160/share was approximately a 10% discount to where ACMS shares were trading at the time. Below is the capitalization table of ACMR:

Below is a table laying out the value of ACMR’s holdings in ACMS and the implied upside for ACMR shares:

So the massive difference between ACMR’s market capitalization ($3.6b) versus ACMS’s ($11.1b) implies 127% upside from current levels, or $115/share.

Thus far, this has all been a somewhat theoretical discussion, which is partly why the huge valuation discrepancy between the two market-derived valuations has persisted. But in reality, this discrepancy can be resolved through corporate restructuring and financial engineering, and there are paths where ACMR shareholders could actually get $115 in cash, or some similarly much higher number than current trading levels, for their shares, and if the path to this occurring became clear, we anticipate ACMR shares would rocket upwards towards fair value.

We’ve long advocated that the most sensible route to achieving fair value involves a listing on an Asian exchange, preferably Hong Kong, because it’s the most liquid and most accessible to U.S. shareholders. We’ve advocated this ever since our first article on ACMR in February 2025 and pitched this to management, who we found receptive to the concept.

On Friday, the path to a listing in Hong Kong may have begun its journey from theory to reality, and the implications for ACMR shares are potentially tremendous.

Why Is a Hong Kong Listing Potentially So Powerful

Fundamentally, ACMR and ACMS should trade in line with one another—they both represent equity in essentially the same operations, sector trends, and management. Instead, ACMR trades at a 56% discount to ACMS shares.

One could argue that listed holding companies trade at a discount to listed operating companies, but that discount is typically 10-20%, not 56%.

One can also argue that companies trading exclusively in China can be overvalued because China restricts outside investment in its market and restricts mainland Chinese investors from investing abroad. But that can only be used to explain a modest premium for A-share valuations, not a 127% premium. The market access restrictions are partial instead of absolute. For foreign investors buying Chinese stocks, access is allowed through controlled channels, even if the Chinese stock markets are not fully open to all foreigners. The main official routes are the Qualified Foreign Investor (QFI) regime and Northbound Stock Connect through Hong Kong. Mainland Chinese investors are also restricted from investing in foreign exchanges, and while there are quotas allowed for Qualified Domestic Institutional Investors, these restrictions help explain the premium that Chinese shares can trade at.

But that Chinese A-share valuation premium can be attributed to being around 10-30%, not 127%. This article from last June in Bloomberg provides a good analysis:

Stocks listed on mainland exchanges, known as A-shares, are now trading at a 27% premium to their counterparts across the border, according to the Hang Seng Stock Connect China AH Premium Index… In the case of some stocks favored by global investors like BYD Co. and Contemporary Amperex Technology Co., A-shares are trading at a rare discount to H-shares, according to calculations by Bloomberg.

There’s actually an index called the Hang Seng Stock Connect China AH Premium Index that tracks the Chinese stock market’s valuation premium calculated by comparing the A-shares to H-Shares of an index of companies. With 100 being par, a trading value above 100 implies an A-shares premium, and a trading value below 100 implies an A-share discount. The current 118 trading price implies a current A-share premium of only 18%.

An 18% premium for ACMS would imply an ACMR share price of $97, nearly double current levels.

So an OpCo/HoldCo premium and a Chinese stock market valuation premium don’t explain why ACMS trades a staggering 127% higher than ACMR.

The answer, in our opinion, has always been more related to ACMR’s listing in the United States, given that the company operates in the geopolitically sensitive semiconductor capital equipment sector. And a Hong Kong listing fixes that.

Let’s Go to Hong Kong!

ACMR’s U.S. listing is a historical artifact from a bygone era. The company itself was formed in the United States by Founder/CEO David Wang more than 30 years ago, and it went public in 2017. Back then, geopolitical tensions between the United States and China over the semiconductor sector were in their infancy and were little discussed in political and market circles. It’s been mainly in the past 5 years that U.S. regulators have stepped up policy actions to curb China’s semiconductor sector growth. In turn, China’s own aggressive push to localize all aspects of semiconductor production—which is also the massive macro tailwind driving ACMR’s operational growth—really accelerated after COVID, directly in response to U.S. rhetoric and regulatory measures.

As a result, while ACMR’s Delaware corporation and U.S. listing made perfect sense in 2017—back when Chinese tech companies traded at healthy valuation multiples if they listed on NASDAQ—they no longer make sense today. Chinese companies with primary listings in the U.S. began to pursue dual listings in Hong Kong, like Alibaba (2019), NIO (2022), and Tencent Music (2022). It’s now ACMR’s turn to do so.

We think that it is ACMR’s corporate domicile in Delaware and its U.S. listing—with no available tradeable Hong Kong shares—that account for ACMR’s massive discount to ACMS.

Our original idea that we pitched to ACM Research management is that the company pursue a dual listing of its ACMR shares in Hong Kong. Based on the 8K, it appears that the company is instead pursuing a dual-listing for its ACMS shares. We’re interested to hear the company’s rationale for pursuing an ACMS listing as opposed to an ACMR listing, but, nevertheless, both seem like workable pathways to close the huge valuation discrepancy between ACMR and ACMS. Indeed, either option would develop the main thing we need: sufficiently liquid Hong Kong-listed shares fully accessible to U.S. shareholders.

Once such shares exist and are available, there could be a variety of pathways for U.S. shareholders to “convert” their U.S.-listed shares to Hong Kong-listed shares. Maybe the company does an exchange offer allowing ACMR holders to convert their holding company shares to operating company ACMS-HK shares—let's call these new potential Hong Kong-listed ACMS shares “ACMS-HK.” Maybe ACMS-HK does public offerings and uses proceeds to buy back ACMR shares—essentially, the company would be issuing shares at $115 and doing share buybacks at $50 (or, more realistically, they’d probably issue shares at a 10-20% discount to $115 and perhaps do tender offers at a 20% premium to $50). While there are a variety of scenarios, the company and its investment bankers could come up with creative ways to create a gravitational force that’ll pull up the valuation multiple of ACMR to converge much closer to that of ACMS.

We anticipate the company will begin providing further clues on its thinking in upcoming investor communications. But Friday’s 8K is a big deal. To us, it signifies that management—after many months of exploring how to utilize a Hong Kong listing to collapse the massive valuation discrepancy between ACMR and ACMS—has come up with ideas that it is ready to actively pursue. We also encourage other shareholders to offer up creative plans on how a Hong Kong listing could be used to unlock value for the U.S.-listed shares. Over the long term, we believe that the goal should be for ACMR to primarily trade in Hong Kong and mainland China, not the United States and mainland China, and we anticipate that Hong Kong-listed shares would trade at no more than a 30% discount to A-Shares. In fact, since ACMR is an exciting operational growth story with tremendous thematic tailwinds, we anticipate that Hong Kong-listed shares would trade at only a modest 10-15% discount to the A-shares. ACMR will remain one of the best ways to invest in China's domestic localization of its semiconductor industry for many years to come.

The implied upside for ACMR from the brief disclosure at the end of last week is very significant. Shares were up 6% on Friday in trading following the 8K. We think they should be up much more.

Q4 and 2025 Earnings Reports

The company reported earnings on February 26. Revenue grew 9% and 15% in Q4 and 2025, respectively, as the company continues to benefit from China's active investment into its domestic semiconductor industry. The company's midpoint guidance for 2026 implies 25% revenue growth this year. In addition to the continued buildout of China's memory and logic foundries, ACMR is also benefiting from China's own push to build out chip development aimed at AI development.

data.title: Almonty Industries: Memory Supercycle And Iran War Cause Tungsten Shortage, Making This Stock A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

At the center of the escalation of warfare in the Middle East and the semiconductor supercycle is tungsten—a metal of strategic importance. China, which historically controlled over 80% of global supply, is now implementing a protectionist policy on tungsten. Consequently, prices for tungsten have increased by over 500% since it was added to China’s export control list.

I am initiating coverage on Almonty Industries Inc. (ALM) with a Strong Buy rating. My investment thesis on ALM hinges on a supply crunch while demand is exploding due to export controls, increased memory demand & recent conflicts, a strong Western positioning with all mines located in Western-aligned countries, and an excellent distressed asset strategy focused on optimizing past-producing mines rather than high-risk greenfield exploration.

Market Backdrop

Tungsten’s physical profile, with the highest melting point of any metal at 3,422 degrees Celsius and a density of 19.3 grams per cubic centimeter, makes it ideal for use cases in defense and semiconductors, among other sectors. Another crucial tungsten property is its electrical conductivity while maintaining stability. Although it is not as conductive as copper, it is much more stable under stress, allowing for the prevention of failures in tiny interconnects & the maintenance of a reliable signal flow over time.

As shown in the chart above, China accounted for over 80% of global supply and more than half of known reserves in 2024. For decades, the global market relied on a steady flow of Chinese material, which kept prices suppressed and discouraged Western investment. This changed in February 2025 when China implemented aggressive export licensing requirements, followed in January 2026 by the "Catalogue of Dual-Use Items". Furthermore, China has reduced quotas for mining tungsten for 3 years by 6% each year.

Tungsten is used for high-density projectiles that use momentum rather than explosives to destroy hardened targets and underground bunkers. The Tomahawk missile, for example, which is partially composed of tungsten, has been used in the Iran War – over 500 Tomahawk missiles were deployed. According to RUSI, over 11,000 munitions were used in just the first 16 days of the Iran War, and consequently, there have been reports that US missile stockpiles have been significantly depleted.

While the Iran War has created more of a short-term spike in military demand, the semiconductor industry is driving a long-term structural supercycle due to the proliferation of AI. In the fabrication of advanced integrated circuits, tungsten is used as a contact and via material to link different layers of the chip. As the industry moves toward <2 nm nodes, the density of these tungsten vias increases. Furthermore, in 3D NAND flash memory, where storage cells are stacked vertically, tungsten is used for wordlines (control wires that select which memory cells you’re accessing) and vertical connections because it can be applied as a gas (tungsten hexafluoride).

TrendForce, a Taiwanese market intelligence firm focused on technology industry reports, states,

Recent media reports indicate that some Japanese suppliers of tungsten hexafluoride (WF₆) have notified Korean semiconductor companies of possible disruptions in raw material supply, with existing inventories expected to last only until mid-2026. Industry observers warn that such supply constraints could drive up chip manufacturing costs, delay mass production of advanced-node devices, and further pass on to downstream, potentially leading to product shortages and price increases in end markets.

Western Champion Positioning

ALM has positioned itself as the Western producer of tungsten, operating in geopolitically stable, conflict-free jurisdictions like Spain, Portugal, South Korea, and the US that are directly aligned with the defense and technology priorities of the West. ALM is also shifting its corporate headquarters to the US and appointed several former senior US Army generals as directors in order to capitalize on US government agencies and defense contractors.

In my opinion, the Sangdong mine is ALM’s flagship asset and the key growth driver. It is widely considered one of the most significant tungsten deposits in the world. ALM, thus far, has finished the first phase of commissioning at the Sangdong mine. The mine has an average ore grade of 0.51% tungsten trioxide, which is 3x the global average. The mine also has a projected mine life of over 45 years. Within Phase 1, the mine is designed to handle about 640,000 tonnes of ore annually, with a yield of almost 2,300 tonnes of tungsten concentrate per year.

ALM expanded into the US by acquiring the Gentung Browns Lake project in Montana. The Gentung project is on track for production readiness by H2 2026. This is a past-producing mine that ALM plans to redevelop. Gentung is expected to produce roughly 140,000 MTUs per year when it’s at full capacity.

While the Sangdong and Gentung projects are the growth drivers, ALM’s European operations in Portugal provide a stable foundation of production and cash flow. ALM is currently undergoing expansion within the Portuguese Panasqueira mine, with the expansion projected to increase annual output to approximately 124,000 MTUs.

Almonty’s Excellent Distressed Asset Strategy

The core of ALM’s strategy is its focus on past-producing mines, operations on care and maintenance, and tailings stockpiles. By targeting assets with known mineralization and established infrastructure, the company significantly lowers its geological risk. Management states that their focus is on situations where near-term production and positive cash flow can be achieved through technical optimization and capital infusion.

For example, the Sangdong mine was not a new discovery. Its geology was well-documented during decades of previous operations. ALM’s role was to acquire the project from Woulfe Mining Corp. in 2015, secure the necessary $75 million in project financing from KfW IPEX-Bank, and apply modern mining engineering to bring it back online. In my opinion, this approach is excellent because it does not carry the same discovery risk that often wipes out investors in greenfield projects.

Once an asset is acquired, ALM utilizes its expertise to optimize the performance of the distressed asset. At Panasquiera, for example, ALM has successfully transitioned the mine into a mechanized room-and-pillar operation to reduce labor costs. At Sangdong, the company has invested over $100 million in four kilometers of new underground tunnel development and a mineral processing plant.

Financial Performance

I believe ALM’s financial statements show a company transitioning from a multi-year construction phase into a revenue-generating phase. In Q4 2025, ALM reported $6.4 million in revenue—this is an increase of 3% on a quarter-over-quarter basis and an increase of 45% on a year-over-year basis. Gross profit margins have also improved to 17.94% from -3.71% in Q4 2024. However, this is not the full top-line story—much of it is forward-looking. I expect there to be a huge ramp-up in revenue driven by both pricing increases and capacity coming online.

Above is the evolution of tungsten pricing data sourced from the ALM website. There is a clear upwards trend—from March 13 to April 10, there is a 35% increase in the average price per MTU. Therefore, for my assumptions that follow below, I am going to use different price levels to forecast revenue. I, personally, believe that tungsten prices are going to continue increasing, especially due to the factors discussed previously within the “Market Backdrop” section.

The bear case utilizes average pricing from March 2026, the base case is based on highs from April 2026, and the bull case represents a further 25% increase in prices from April highs. Utilizing the estimated amount of MTUs forecasted by ALM & the price forecasts, we can reasonably estimate the amount of additional revenue generated by mines.

In 2026 alone, an additional $466 million to $830 million is expected to be brought in from the Sangdong and Gentung mines. In 2027, a further $2 billion to $3.65 billion is expected in revenue from significant expansion to ALM’s mines. It is also important to note that for the molybdenum project, the prices are calculated off spot prices.

Moving on to the balance sheet, as of EOY 2025, ALM held $195.6 million in cash and equivalents. This significant liquidity position was achieved through two major public offerings in 2025: a $90 million Nasdaq IPO in July and a $129.4 million follow-on offering in December. This represents an increase in cash by over 36x since Q4 2024. This cash position also ensures that the company is fully funded for its Phase 2 expansion at Sangdong and the restart of Gentung in Montana without requiring additional capital in the near term.

Valuation

ALM’s valuation is highly sensitive to the price of tungsten. While the company’s offtake agreements provide hard floors for protection, they also allow for participation in spot market upside. Looking at the multiples shown in the picture above, it is clear that all typical valuation multiples look elevated. However, much of this is due to expectations of continued price increases in tungsten and forward-looking revenue from mines yet to come online.

At the current $6.6 billion market cap, ALM trades at just 1.6x of the 2027 revenue bull case. I consider this a very reasonable place to buy ALM, especially considering the memory supercycle, Chinese export controls, and the depletion of missiles from the Iran War. I believe the shortage of tungsten to not be a temporary fad but a structural, long-lasting crunch.

Risks

The Phase 2 expansion carries technical and engineering risks. Any delay in production ramp-up could negatively affect the company’s revenue targets. Furthermore, the current price elevations in tungsten are largely driven by Chinese export controls. A sudden shift in Chinese trade policy could cause tungsten prices to retreat from their record highs.

Conclusion

To conclude, I believe that the tungsten shortage caused by missile stockpile depletions, the memory supercycle, and Chinese export controls will lead to further appreciation in the price of tungsten. To capitalize on this tungsten shortage, I believe that ALM is the most compelling tungsten miner investment opportunity because of their high exposure to western tungsten mines, a superior distressed asset acquisition strategy, and near-term revenue exposure from mines coming online soon.

“Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Commodity Idea investment competition, which runs through May 31. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!”

data.title: Canadian Inflation Comments
data.description:
So far, the gasoline price shock in Canada has not been as bad as what happened after the pandemic, but I am not incredibly optimistic about the medium-term outlook.

The above figure is the national average cost for unleaded gasoline (at self-service stations if you want to get even more specific), but the data ends in March, so it is missing the price rise since then.

In my neck of the woods in the Greater Montreal Area, the pump price has been around $2 per litre, although Montreal is normally higher than the national average series shown above.

The Carney government announced a temporary suspension of the excise tax on gasoline on April 14th, which dropped the price by about 11 cents per litre. This generated a lot of flak from economists, but I doubt that it will matter that much if the situation does not improve in the Middle East.

The Canadian CPI numbers for March were released today, and core inflation remains stuck at a level above the 2% target. This starting point makes it hard for the Bank of Canada to be too complacent in the face of commodity prices.

The diplomatic situation around the Strait of Hormuz seems muddled, but it is clear that full traffic flow will not resume immediately. This means that oil and gas fields will be shut in for an extended period, and restarting them will take time.

As such, it is clear that regions that are dependent upon the commodity flows from the Gulf are going to be hit hard. Canada is not directly affected, but it will see a lagged effect of slowing growth elsewhere.

At the same time, the signals from the White House remain belligerent towards Canada, and an attack on the Canada/Mexico/United States trade pact may occur once attention is drawn again to that topic.

The Bank of Canada’s next scheduled policy rate announcement is April 29th, and I see no reason for them to switch from their last assessment that growth risks are skewed to the downside, while inflation risks are to the upside. In the absence of clear data, it seems unlikely that they will move in the near run.

Fiscal Policy?

Although the gasoline excise tax cut was unpopular with economists, it is still a limited measure that targets the fastest-rising price in the economy. My uneducated guess is that fiscal policy is going to deviate much from already announced plans.

There are currently no measurable growth risks that will cause panic loosening of policy. A global recession due to the commodity shock would take time to hit Canada (and as a commodity exporter, some sectors of the economy will benefit from higher commodity prices). A complete rupture of free trade with the United States is a scenario that might provoke a more rapid fiscal reaction.

Without accommodative fiscal policy, the commodity price hike will tend to squeeze consumers, and so there might not be second-round price effects. (Although Canadian commodity exports would benefit, the employment in primary industries is not large enough to greatly push the overall labour market.)

data.title: Silver And Gold Intraday Outlook: Metals In Focus With Ceasefire Uncertainty
data.description:
By Elior Manier

After rebounding sporadically over the past two weeks, gold and other precious commodities have failed to match the broadly positive, euphoric mood currently driving equity markets.

While they initially profited from the first corrective wave in the US dollar, capital flows have aggressively pivoted toward risk-on assets.

Traditional safe havens, which arguably lost their clear directional sight during the height of the war's panic, are now heavily questioning their role in this new environment - and investors are doing exactly the same.

The geopolitical clock is ticking. The temporary US-Iran ceasefire is officially set to expire by Wednesday, April 22, and both sides appear increasingly reluctant to extend the truce without a finalized, signed agreement in place.

The US administration is eager for a peaceful resolution, but the president communicated that he is also ready to use strength. Despite crude oil dropping back to the $90 handle, metals are struggling to pick up any meaningful bullish momentum while in this tense waiting room.

Should the ceasefire unexpectedly collapse without a diplomatic resolution, precious metals could face a violent, binary reaction. If WTI oil manages to remain contained below the critical $100 mark, gold could absolutely explode higher on a sudden rush of risk-off haven flows, capitalizing on its recent technical correction, and a lack of worsening inflation expectations.

Conversely, more risk-sensitive, industrial-leaning metals like silver and copper would likely struggle to catch a sustained bid, facing heavy downward pressure precisely because they are hovering near their current relative highs.

Let's explore the recent shifts in an intraday timeframe analysis of gold (XAU/USD) and silver (XAG/USD) to identify where the key levels are to watch for breakouts.

Gold (XAU/USD) 4H Chart and levels

Gold has rallied a quite impressive 17% after reaching 4-month lows on March 22 but has failed to breach the quintessential $4,900 resistance.

For bulls to retake the intermediate momentum advantage, they will have to generate a proper push above the psychological level – that could potentially happen if sentiment sours further.

In the immediate outlook, however, the 4H 200-period MA is putting bearish pressure; hence, if nothing fundamental changes, sellers would have the short-term advantage.

A break below the $4,781 50 MA confirms a turn lower and would push back towards $4,650.

Intraday timeframe levels to watch for gold (XAU/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • $4,800 4H 200-period MA
  • $4,850 to $4,900 major resistance (bullish above)
  • $5,100 pivotal resistance
  • $5,400 mini resistance

Support Levels:

  • $4,781 50-MA short-term support
  • Daily momentum pivot $4,675 (bearish below)
  • Pivotal support: $4,325-$4,400
  • Main channel lows support: $4,100

Silver (XAG/USD) 4H Chart and levels

Silver has also rallied strongly from its March 22 lows but is now struggling to extend above the $83 resistance. Still evolving within a bull channel, traders will have to track its upper ($84.50) and lower bounds ($77) to play breakouts.

If the situation remains as confusing as it currently is, expect the channel to consolidate into a range between $77 and $83.

Higher timeframe levels to watch for silver (XAG/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • Major resistance: $83 to $84.50 (mid-term bullish above)
  • Key range resistance: $90 to $92
  • $96.47 March highs (higher odds of all-time highs if break above)

data.title: Wise plc: Valuation Concerns Outweigh Benefits Of Upcoming NASDAQ Listing
data.description:
About this time last year, I wrote my first analysis of Wise plc (WIZEY), calling the stock a Buy. My opinion was based on the company’s potential for market share growth in its industry due to several macroeconomic factors, as well as my own experiences as a user of its services. Since that article was published on April 8, WIZEY has gained just over 20% in value, which is nothing to lose sleep about, except that the S&P 500 has appreciated about twice that amount during the same time period.

A new potential tailwind for the company is set to arrive next month as the UK-based financial services company expects to start trading on the NASDAQ marketplace on May 11. The listing will change almost nothing about the operations of the company itself, but it does open up the stock to potential higher trading volumes and more institutional investments from US firms. Wise also released its Q4 2026 Trading Update on April 13, giving me reason to revisit my initial research.

The company is showing impressive growth in active customers and account holdings. It seems to be continuously improving the speed of its currency transfers and decreasing costs for users. My biggest concern for the stock is its valuation, which seems to be slightly elevated in comparison to many of its peers. The market in which it operates is also incredibly competitive, which could cause it to drop the price of its services even more to keep adding users to its platform. As a Wise customer, I am still quite pleased with the value I receive for the services that I receive, but as an investor, I am somewhat less enthusiastic about buying more of its shares at their current price.

Company Overview

Founded in 2011 under the name TransferWise, Wise plc changed its name in 2021 to reflect its range of services that go just beyond the movement of currencies. The company now reports its operations in three separate divisions—Wise Personal, Wise Business, and Wise Platform. Wise Personal allows individuals to send and receive money in 160 countries and hold money in 40 different currencies. Wise also offers consumers the ability to use a multi-currency debit card that converts currencies at the mid-market rate.

Wise Business is for small and medium-sized businesses that need to send, receive, or hold money. It also offers accounting tools and payment options not available to individuals. Some funds held in Wise Business accounts may have the option of FDIC insurance through a pass-through program at partner banks. However, it is important to note that Wise plc itself is not a bank and does not offer any consumer or commercial lending services.

Wise Platform is a fintech solution for businesses that want to integrate Wise’s currency tools into their own applications. Wise plc has impressive partnerships in place with major financial players like Morgan Stanley (MS), Nu Holdings (NU), and Standard Chartered (SCBFY). In 2024, Morgan Stanley selected Wise plc to facilitate foreign exchange settlements for its corporate clients. In the same year, Nu Holdings, which operates under the name Nu Bank, announced that it would use Wise Platform to issue multi-currency accounts and debit cards.

The company’s biggest selling point is its ability to offer rapid currency transfers at low costs to its users. Almost 15 million customers and clients use Wise plc’s services every day. Almost three-fourths of the transfers it processes arrive at their account destination in under 20 seconds, and 95% arrive on the same day. The company highlights the fact that it uses mid-market exchange rates to lower the costs of its transfers. An example from its website on how a transfer of $1000 US dollars to euros would compare with Bank of America (BAC), Wells Fargo (WFC), and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) is shown below.

WIZEY vs. WPLCF

For the purpose of this analysis, I am writing about WIZEY. However, there are two ways for US investors to currently invest in Wise plc. The first is the US ADR, WIZEY. The second is the less liquid version of the stock, the ordinary shares that have the symbol WPLCF. Because both are currently traded on the OTC markets, there may be added costs for buying and selling shares in a US-based brokerage account. WPLCF has a lower average trading volume and may have wider bid-ask spreads.

At the time I am writing this analysis, WIZEY shares trade at about a 6% premium to WPLCF. Over the last three years, the ADR has outperformed the ordinary shares by about 10%. I would recommend limit orders for a transaction for either stock, at least until WIZEY is listed on a US exchange, but extra caution is advised for any investors trying to trade WPLCF. A market order for the ordinary shares could settle at a price notably higher or lower than the last transaction.

The Case for WIZEY

Because Wise plc is not yet traded in the US, it does not report its financial results in the same way that a domestic company would. The company releases detailed financial reports every six months. WIZEY released a Q4 FY2026 Trading Update on April 13 that contained the following information for the first three months of this calendar year:

  • Underlying income, or adjusted income, was approximately $588.2 million (£435.3 million), an increase of 24% year-over-year.
  • The company’s income is continuing to become more diversified as customer holdings grew by 37%, while card and other revenue rose 29% year-over-year.
  • The number of active Wise business customers grew by 26%, compared to the same period last year, to 572,000.
  • Q4 cross-border currency volume increased 26% to $59.5 billion (£44 billion) with 11.3 million active customers.

More detailed progress in these areas can be seen in the chart below.

Looking back at the last full report from the company, which it called its HY 2026 results, all of the company’s growth is not falling to its bottom line at the same rate. The company’s pre-tax margin fell 5.9% in the first half of FY 2026 and was lower than in the first half of FY 2024. Net profit before taxes had grown 51% between the first six months of FY 2024 and the same period of FY 2025. In the first half of FY 2026, it had fallen 13%.

The cause of this change is an increase in company expenses. General and administrative investments increased 35% from the first half of FY 2025 to the same period in FY 2026. Tech and development investments increased 18% during this time period. Marketing and sales costs ballooned 59% in the first half of FY 2026 compared to the same period from the previous year. In short, the company is paying more to run its business, invest for the future, and attract new customers. Many of these investments may very well pay off with greater sales and profits in the future, but there are no guarantees.

Peer Comparison

Wise plc operates in a very competitive market. For my peer comparison, I used the following five companies that are all involved in similar currency services. One from this group, Western Union (WU), still supports a significant physical network, but the company has become more involved in digital transactions in recent years.

  • Global Payments (GPN)
  • Payoneer Global (PAYO)
  • PayPal Holdings (PYPL)
  • Remitly Global (RELY)
  • Western Union

The first thing that I noticed is how differently these companies are treated by the market. I expected PayPal to attract the most attention, but each of these companies has a minimum of 10 analysts who cover their stocks. WIZEY has none.

Wise plc appears to currently trade at an elevated valuation based on a number of different financial metrics. Its PE ratio for the last 12 months of reported earnings is not too far out of range, but the difference would have been much greater if RELY’s valuation of 64.6 times earnings was removed from the equation. Western Union is currently valued at just 6.2. PayPal has a PE ratio of 9.39.
