With other metrics like price to sales and enterprise value to sales, which were calculated on forward data, WIZEY is twice as expensive as its peer group. For price-to-book ratio, Wise’s closest peer is 4.85 from Remitly Global. Not all of these ratios hold equal weight in my book for what the stock from Wise is truly worth, but it is just hard to find any great justification for its current stock price when looking at other companies that conduct similar business operations.

Risks to Consider

Competition in the fintech payments space is fierce, with a number of players who have greater financial resources than Wise plc. The company will be under constant pressure to innovate and stay ahead of the competition. Innovation and advancements in technology create added expenses for a company that is already seeing pressure on its profit growth. Increased use of digital currencies could also impact the company, as it currently has a policy that does not allow the purchase or trading of cryptocurrency assets. Wise does not allow customers to send money to crypto wallets, receive money from peer-to-peer crypto trading, or hold money on behalf of individuals or businesses that trade virtual currencies.

An overall slowdown in the world economy, which could be caused by trade conflicts, logistical issues caused by the current situation in the Middle East, or any other number of macro headwinds, could impact the bottom line for WIZEY. Declining economic activity could lead to a decrease in the demand for the company’s currency transfer services. It could also cause current customers to pull money out of their Wise accounts.

The company is also adding a small bit of risk with its upcoming NASDAQ listing. There will be greater compliance standards for the company and added fees to meet new legal and accounting standards. Should WIZEY fall out of compliance with any of these standards, its listing could be suspended, damaging the company’s reputation.

Conclusion

The recent updates on Wise plc's operations and its listing in the US have had a clear impact on the stock. WIZEY has gained almost 23% in the last month. It has increased over 12% in value over just the last week. The stock could see additional short-term gains heading into May, similar to the impact that a stock in the US could see if it were to be added to one of the major indices. The fundamentals of the company are not changing, however, which is why I believe any new short-term pop in the price of the stock could be met with a reversion to the mean over the next several weeks after it is listed.

I am still a believer in the company as a customer, and I am still holding on to the relatively small amount of shares that I do own myself for now. The company is continuing to grow and innovate in a market that can change in a relatively short period of time. I just do not see enough supporting evidence to add to my position; hence, my rating change to a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ECB Waits For Signals From The Economy. War With Iran Raises Risks To Inflation And Growth In The Euro Area
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

The European Central Bank is still unable to clearly assess how strong the full impact of the war with Iran will be on the euro area economy. As Álvaro Santos Pereira, a member of the ECB’s Governing Council, emphasizes, the conflict is too recent to draw firm conclusions, so for now the central bank should closely monitor incoming data and refrain from hasty reactions.

Too early for a full assessment of the conflict’s effects

According to Pereira, the current situation requires caution because this is a classic supply shock. Such shocks usually lead at the same time to weaker economic growth and higher inflation, putting the central bank in a particularly difficult position.

On the one hand, energy and commodity prices are rising; on the other, economic activity is weakening. For the euro area, this means worsening conditions, although at this stage the negative impact of the conflict is not yet seen as dramatic.

A supply shock puts the ECB in a difficult position

Pereira notes that the euro area economy is currently somewhere between the ECB’s baseline scenario and the adverse scenario it had considered. Even before the current crisis, economic growth in the euro area had been running at around 1%, which in itself pointed to limited recovery momentum.

In this situation, any additional external shock, especially one related to the energy market and geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, increases the risk of a further weakening in economic conditions.

The ECB is paying particularly close attention to developments surrounding the fighting in the Middle East and the potential consequences for Europe of a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. This channel could be of key importance for oil prices, transport costs, and more broadly for cost pressures in the economy.

For the central bank, however, the most important issue will not be the temporary rise in prices itself, but the possibility of so-called second-round effects. This refers to a situation in which higher energy and commodity prices begin to spread into other sectors of the economy, pushing inflation higher in a more lasting way.

The biggest risk is entrenched inflationary pressure

In Pereira’s view, only clear signs that higher inflation is becoming entrenched, along with rising inflation expectations, should prompt the ECB to react. If such signals appear in the data, the central bank will have to respond. If, however, price pressure proves limited and temporary, it will be more appropriate to continue observing the situation and make decisions with great caution. This is particularly important at a time when less than two weeks remained before the ECB’s next interest-rate decision.

Europe needs not only caution, but also reforms

In the Governing Council member’s opinion, short-term caution in monetary policy should not obscure the broader picture. Pereira points out that for more lasting growth, Europe also needs structural measures, above all faster completion of the single market. Deeper economic integration could increase Europe’s resilience to external shocks and improve its long-term growth prospects.

For now, the ECB therefore remains in a mode of vigilant observation. The conflict with Iran is already worsening economic conditions in the euro area, but the scale of its impact has not yet been determined. The coming weeks and incoming data will show whether this is a temporary disruption or the start of stronger and more persistent inflationary pressure that would force the central bank to respond.

Original Post

data.title: Axon Enterprise: Impressive Growth, Real Margin Work Left
data.description:
Shares of Axon Enterprise (AXON) have lost half their value from a peak over the $800 mark in the summer. Despite such a pullback, shares have seen flattish returns since the early autumn of 2024, when I concluded that investors should not get tasered.

This pullback is seen in response to the company having posted record sales results recently, having demonstrated impressive growth in 2025. While this growth and strong sales guidance is comforting, lack of realistic profits due to excessive stock-based compensation expenses is a real issue, although such costs are seen coming down a bit in 2026, and hopefully in the coming years.

With shares cut in half, sales multiples remain demanding, as greater operating leverage is required to see appeal here, with strong topline momentum expected here. Appeal is far from being seen at this point, yet the pullback looks compelling, with more operating leverage required here.

Other interesting growth compounders are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

A Strong 2025

In February, Axon reported strong results for 2025, at least topline results. Reported sales rose by 33% to $2.78 billion. Product revenue rose by 29% to $1.58 billion, with service revenue up nearly 40% to $1.20 billion. That revenue composition helps in boosting margins, with the cost of services, of course, being much lower.

Despite gross margins being up 34%, the company suffered from higher operating expenses as GAAP operating profit of $58 million in 2024 turned into a loss of $62 million in 2025. The company reported GAAP earnings of $125 million, but the quality of this growth was poor, driven by a tax refund and large other income.

The company posted an adjusted profit of $564 million, or $6.85 per share, but I have real issues with that profitability metric. While I am happy to adjust for some smaller items, a $610 million pre-tax stock-based compensation expense is not among them, amidst minimal heavily adjusted earnings otherwise.

Amidst a flattish net debt load, an outstanding share base of about 83 million shares works down to a +$33 billion enterprise valuation. This is equivalent to about 12 times sales, although a near $800 million revenue number for the fourth quarter already trends at $3.2 billion, reducing the sales multiple to 10 times, amidst essentially break-even results reported.

Continued Growth

For 2026, the company calls for growth to continue at an impressive pace. Sales are seen up 27-30%, with adjusted EBITDA margins seen around 25.5% of sales. At the midpoint of the guidance, that suggests adjusted EBITDA of $911 million, which is ironic given the nature of the activities, compared to a $710 million number in 2025.

Comforting is that stock-based compensation expenses are seen as flattish around $605 million; this number is actually down minimally from a $610 million number in 2025, revealing that real profits are seen here. This is very encouraging, in the sense that real profits are seen here, these, however, being minimal in relation to a $33 billion enterprise valuation, even after shares cut in half from last year's highs.

Based on the guidance, sales are expected to surpass $3.5 billion this year, reducing the sales multiple to about 9 times here, and non-meaningful earnings multiples far exceed a hundred times here.

Safety Technology, Not Necessarily A Safe Investment

Axon's mission is to transform public safety by the usage of technology. The vision includes a world without bullets, a reduction in social conflict, and people having access to a fair and effective justice system.

The company aims to do this with its well-known Taser system, a less-lethal option compared to guns, and other services, including body cameras, in-car cameras, drones, and related software. Most of this is driven by the US, where the company generates 85% of sales in 2024, a number gradually seen coming down to 81% in the final quarter of 2025.

Founder-led, Axon has seen strong growth in the past. Over the past decade, revenues have increased tenfold here, with the company managing growth with margins that are essentially seen near break-even points, although the company has incurred about 50% dilution over this period of time.

Even down 50% from the highs, valuation multiples have expanded a great deal, having increased by more than 20 times over the past decade, indicating real valuation multiple expansion despite shares cut in half. Shares have fallen to levels first seen in the fall of 2024. Shares have been falling relentlessly recently but are up meaningfully in the long haul, as investors are catching up with reality, and they are not necessarily buying into the AI strategy employed by the business.

What Now?

Compared to the situation in September 2024, when I was cautious amidst nonexistent realistic earnings, something has changed here. At the time, the company traded at 14 times annualized sales; this multiple has come down to 10 times here, or 9 times if we look at the guidance for the year. Amidst no realistic profits seen here, expectations remain high, but multiples have come down quite a bit since 2024.

So here we are, with still no realistic earnings to be reported, but this is about to change for the better based on the 2026 guidance, with stock-based compensation seen as flat amidst impressive topline sales growth. That is comforting, but those earnings are still too minimal to provide support to the investment case. On the other hand, it is comforting to see the midpoint of stock-based compensation expenses being doled out amidst continued sales growth and dominance in the field.

This makes me more upbeat compared to the situation at equivalent levels nearly two years ago, yet that is just a minimal improvement in relation to a valuation that is still highly demanding. Valuations are still too demanding for me to buy a 50% dip from a too-far momentum-induced run higher before. However, there is more clarity to the future, with Axon reporting a strong 46% increase in 2025 bookings to $7.4 billion, adding rapidly to the backlog.

Besides, the company has provided strong 2028 revenue guidance, which calls for revenues around $6 billion. With sales seen near $3.6 billion in 2026, that suggests average revenue growth around 30% in both 2027 and 2028, in line with the guidance given this year and growth reported last year. This makes me upbeat, yet EBITDA margins are only seen up about 250 basis points in 2028, which only tells us part of the story as the question of how stock-based compensation will evolve. On that front, the company sees about 2.5% dilution each year in relation to the value of the firm, but that is not really telling that much.

After all, a current $33 billion enterprise valuation implies $800 million in stock-based compensation expenses, up from around $600 million actually guided this year. All in all, impressive topline growth is seen, yet operating leverage appears to be modest in the year to come, making me hard-pressed to see immediate appeal.

data.title: Digital Realty: Winning By Losing AI Buildout
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The way the AI infrastructure buildout has unraveled has shaped the sector's risk/reward distribution. When demand surged, it was the wildcatters of the cryptocurrency market that contributed heavily to the buildout - the bosses of the digital currency mining companies, who I dare say have a disposition for risk-taking. CoreWeave (CRWV), the largest pure-play generative AI hyperscaler, traces its roots to crypto mining. Hut 8 (HUT), HIVE Digital (HIVE), Core Scientific (CORZ), and many others have shifted their business models from crypto mining to high-performance computing. Nebius's CEO noted that while hyperscalers such as Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOGL) might have more GPUs, their count is in the hundreds of thousands, putting them in the same category as Nebius.

Beyond the noise of these small risk-takers leading the AI buildout lies Digital Realty (DLR), an established data center REIT with a long track record. DLR has demonstrated underwriting discipline, opting not to chase growth at any margin.

Although demand for computing capacity exceeds supply, what should be a seller's market hasn't translated to favorable unit economics for suppliers. That's because of the unique structure of the market, which I've discussed in previous articles (here and here) and will touch on in the coming paragraphs. But the point here is that raw compute has very slim margins. Neoclouds like Nebius and CoreWeave aren't unprofitable because of R&D investing or construction ramp. R&D is too small, while construction costs are capitalized in the balance sheet. The deep unprofitability of the new infrastructure providers suggests that they might have underwritten unprofitable raw compute contracts (or underestimated costs). CoreWeave emphasizes adjusted EBITDA as its preferred performance metric, which excludes key recurring expenses such as GPU depreciation and interest on debt funding, highlighting its aggressive accounting. Their access to GPUs hasn't translated to high margins because of the unique market structure, characterized by a powerful GPU supplier and established GPU end-users, with neoclouds and smaller infrastructure suppliers squeezed in the middle.

My pitch is simple. Even if the margins of these hyperscalers improve, DLR remains a favorable play because of its discipline. Many of the new HPC providers built their data centers in remote areas where power is cheap. This is marketed as a good thing, particularly after the power supply shortage and the grid stress. Having a data center in a remote area is okay for crypto mining and training a foundational generative AI model, but it's less than ideal when low latency is important. While some of these HPCs are claiming that they're seeing more inference loads, there will come a day when the inference latency becomes unacceptable, and being closer to the urban centers becomes more critical.

For now, people aren't complaining that ChatGPT and Perplexity take 40 seconds to give an answer. They will eventually, and remote data centers won't cut it. This is where DLR's advantage lies. It might not have the triple-digit growth of new market entrants and neoclouds, but that's because of their disciplined approach to the market, focusing on quality locations and reliable, utility-grade power.

Why DLR Deserves a Valuation Premium Over Neoclouds

DLR is losing market share to the new infrastructure providers: Neoclouds and new HPC infrastructure operators. In FY'25, CoreWeave reported 168% revenue growth, while Nebius delivered 479% growth. One might argue that the latter is a full-stack public cloud provider. I would say that's their inspiration, but not reality. At this point, nearly all of Neocloud's revenue comes from raw compute. At this point, the difference between DLR and CRWV/Nebius and others is that one owns the GPUs, and one doesn't.

What's driving the growth of neoclouds is access to power supply and, secondly, GPUs, not the public cloud software services they aspire to sell one day. Here's the point.

Some might point to the "success" of neoclouds in securing energy. Power transmission lines take time to be installed. There is also a long wait for utility-grade gas turbines. Some popular power architecture implemented by Neoclouds includes installing smaller Caterpillar (CAT) diesel and gas combustion engines in parallel, creating a large-capacity cluster. Another emerging solution is the hydrogen fuel cells, particularly the ones sold by Bloom Energy (BE), called the Bloom Box.

These solutions solve the immediate supply/demand imbalance, but they can be more expensive than the utility-grade gas turbines that are currently in short supply. DLR seems to be growing at the same pace as its utility partners, a steady and reliable growth.

Access to GPUs isn't an issue either. I believe that DLR can buy GPUs if it wants to, and I would bet that it can put its hands on the latest GPUs before hyperscalers if it commits to changing its business model from a pure data center REIT. Companies like CoreWeave and Nebius have access to the cutting-edge GPUs because of the frenemies situation between NVIDIA and its hyperscaler customers, most of whom are running their own microchip programs that directly compete with NVIDIA. A significant portion of Amazon's Bedrock cloud services is running on Trainium and Inferentia chips. Anthropic is buying 1 million of Alphabet's TPUs. This is creating tension between NVIDIA and its customers, a frenemies situation that means that NVIDIA is happy to allocate its highly sought-after GPUs to neoclouds and, perhaps, new market entrants like DLR in the unlikely event that it chooses to change its business model from a pure REIT structure. For now, the practical takeaway from these dynamics is my skepticism of any valuation premium over DLR because of the GPU or power access argument.

What's Driving DLR's Growth

DLR's growth is slower but more reliable, fueled by well-established secular trends, including a shift from on-premise server rooms to third-party hosting. Hybrid cloud architectures are on the rise to save costs on public cloud services that charge by the token and instance. For some enterprises, running the core computing load on their own servers while also being connected to the public cloud for certain services saves a lot of money. Some customers are required by law to host their customers' data on their own servers, so they just rent out space from DLR. The key point here is that DLR's customer base is diverse, and each has its own reason to buy rack space. This is contrary to the likes of CoreWeave and Nebius, who derive the majority of their revenue from a handful of hyperscalers. For DLR, only one customer represents more than 10% of revenue - most likely a hyperscaler representing 11.7% of revenue. Beyond that, it is mostly enterprises that entrust DLR to take care of their servers.

Profitability and Dividend Coverage

DLR adopts a capital recycling strategy. The company buys and develops properties, stabilizes them so that they're income-generating, before selling them at a profit. The revenue from this approach is lumpy. However, it does translate to valuable capital gains that management distributes, or, as tax and minimum distribution requirements allow, does reinvest some of the proceeds in growing the business.

The company's adjusted FFO excludes these gains, providing a clear picture of the rent income and reliable run rate. Key items excluded in FFO are depreciation, because, typically, real estate assets increase in value, and depreciation expense increases the gap between real value and GAAP book value. The $2.6 billion in adjusted FFO in 2025 is a decent step up from the $2.2 billion reported in the prior year. This annual run rate is also well above the dividends, which stood at $1.7 billion in 2025, after the dividend increase earlier last year.

Final Thoughts and Risks

DLR has adopted a more disciplined growth strategy, making it one of the most reliable data center infrastructure buildout plays on the market today. Equally important, there are signs that the company's growth is accelerating, fueled by digitalization trends, as enterprises adjust to the new realities created by generative AI. Next year, the company expects AFFO to grow by 8.8%, multiple times its 5-year historical average of 2.4%. This high growth, combined with the broader industry trends, suggests that DLR might be at an inflection point. Booking from <1MW customers rose by 35% at the end of 2025, further strengthening this conclusion.

The main downside risk to this bullish rating is DLR's high valuation. The ticker is up 31% this year, exceeding the growth in its profitability. These dynamics are demonstrated by Seeking Alpha's Quant Score of "F" on valuation. The table below compares DLR's valuation multiples with the Real Estate sector's median, showing a significant premium.

However, one might argue that this premium is justified by growth. DLR has a Quant Score of "A-" on growth, a solid performance compared to peers.

This bullish rating is conditional on the company sustaining its growth momentum and profitability levels. A rating downgrade is possible if dividend coverage ratios deteriorate, threatening the sustainability of distribution.
